channel.title: BLURSOR Research
channel.description: Research on why AI says what it says, distilled for practitioners.

item.title: LLMs mostly source “brand reputation” from other people’s pages
item.description: What LLMs treat as “brand reputation” is mostly other people’s web pages, not the brands themselves

item.title: AI brand “ownership” is moderately concentrated, but the winner changes by model
item.description: Who gets the “top pick” in AI recommendations—and how consistent is it across models?

item.title: English-only AI reputation monitoring misses local champions in multilingual markets
item.description: English-language prompts create a measurable “local-visibility” blind spot across languages

item.title: AI visibility breaks down by entity, not just by mention count
item.description: Why “mention” counts fail: fabricated citations scale differently by entity and query context

item.title: AI search visibility starts with brand stature, not prompt tweaks
item.description: Why the first-run visibility gap between big brands and everyone else is so persistent

item.title: One Polluted Page Is Enough to Hijack LLM Recommendations
item.description: Why one poisoned search result is enough to hijack LLM recommendations

item.title: Safety-trained RAG models can turn a prompt injection into brand suppression
item.description: Why safety alignment can turn retrieval-time injections into brand-level anti-promoters

item.title: ChatGPT referral spikes can overstate AEO unless you control for platform growth
item.description: Why “2x on ChatGPT” stories can be misleading without a tailwind control

item.title: LLMs Score 94% on Cultural Knowledge Tests and 40% When the Answer Choices Are Removed
item.description: When Cultural Knowledge Doesn't Transfer to Cultural Reasoning

item.title: English Prompts Suppress Bengali Cultural Knowledge Even When Local Evidence Is Provided
item.description: How Prompt Language Rewrites Cultural Knowledge Before the Model Even Answers

item.title: LLM Fact-Checkers Score Well But Retrieve the Wrong Sources
item.description: Where LLM Fact-Checkers Go Wrong on Sources

item.title: An LLM Agent That Learns From Its Own Mistakes Beats Human Fact-Checkers on Health Misinformation 89% of the Time
item.description: When the Agent Learns From Its Own Corrections

item.title: AI Overviews Sent Users to Reddit. AI Mode Is Taking Them Back.
item.description: Google AI Overviews drove a 12% rise in Reddit engagement, but AI Mode reversed those gains for experiential communities by substituting conversation for human discussion.

item.title: Ecosystem GEO Beats Page-Level Optimization by Up to 31 Points for Agent Search
item.description: Coordinating a multi-page evidence ecosystem raises LLM search agent recommendation rates by up to 31 percentage points over the best single-page GEO baseline.

item.title: When AI Cites AI: The Synthetic Source Problem in Generative Search
item.description: An audit of ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, and Perplexity finds ~16% of cited sources are AI-generated — with Copilot citing synthetic content in nearly 3 of every 10 citations.

item.title: Frontier LLMs Hallucinate Up to 38% of Scientific Citations
item.description: Six frontier LLMs hallucinate 12–38% of scientific citations; a new agentic retrieval system hits zero hallucination at 30% better F1 and $0.05 per query.

item.title: Rewording a Buying Question Changes the Brands AI Recommends More Than Switching Models Does
item.description: Cosmetic prompt rewording drops AI brand-recommendation overlap by 21–32 percentage points — more divergence than switching providers entirely, across 12,000 runs.

item.title: Query-Specific Expiry: Why 'Recent' Isn't the Same as 'Fresh'
item.description: Baidu's Aurora-Expiry uses RAG-augmented LLMs to infer query-specific expiration thresholds, cutting median document age 12.81% for time-sensitive queries in a 14-day live A/B test.

item.title: RAG Doesn't Flatten the Brand Hierarchy — It Just Moves Where You Lose
item.description: A 37,000-run audit of 533 brands finds RAG preserves the brand hierarchy: L4–L5 specialists face 48–52% invisibility while L1 leaders surface universally but convert at only 25–41%.

item.title: Semantic Metadata Makes Agents More Reliable, Not Smarter
item.description: Schema.org markup gives retrieval agents 65.7% higher FAIR-compliant precision — but cuts query coverage by 29% where publishers haven't adopted it.

item.title: One Bad Search Result Breaks Frontier AI Agents — Completely
item.description: A Microsoft study shows a single false top search result drops GPT-5 accuracy from 65% to 18% — while humans solve the same queries at 93% — exposing a critical gap in agentic RAG deployments.

item.title: Most Pages Get Zero AI Citations. Editing 5% Won 40% More.
item.description: A new paper from Virginia Tech maps four failure modes that prevent pages from being cited in AI-generated responses. 43% of relevant pages receive zero citations under baseline conditions.

item.title: AI Cites Sources It Never Checked — and Half Don't Hold Up
item.description: No LLM verifies even half its citations under any tested condition — and adding temporal cutoffs or other deployment constraints collapses verifiability to near zero.

item.title: Generative Search Citation Share Is a Noisy Estimator, Not a Score
item.description: A new statistical framework shows that single-run citation share metrics from Perplexity, SearchGPT, and Gemini carry confidence intervals wide enough to make most apparent SEO gains statistically indistinguishable from noise.

item.title: Schema Markup Barely Helps AI Find You. Restructuring Does.
item.description: Rewriting pages as entity documents lifted AI answer accuracy ~30%; adding JSON-LD did almost nothing — it gets cut before indexing.
